clinicalStem: A 4-year-old boy is brought by his mother with a 2-day history of right ear pain, fever (38.6°C), and irritability. He has had two similar episodes in the past year. On otoscopy, the right tympanic membrane is erythematous, bulging, and has lost its normal light reflex. There is no mastoid tenderness. He has no known drug allergies. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Oral amoxicillin for 5–7 days
Topical ciprofloxacin ear drops
Immediate myringotomy
Watchful waiting for 48–72 hours only
Intravenous cefuroxime and hospital admission
explanation: Acute otitis media (AOM) with bulging tympanic membrane and systemic features (fever, age <2 or bilateral, or severe symptoms) warrants antibiotic therapy. Oral amoxicillin is first-line for AOM in children. Topical drops do not penetrate an intact tympanic membrane. Myringotomy is reserved for severe or complicated cases. IV antibiotics are only needed for complicated or very severe disease.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Oral amoxicillin is first-line treatment for AOM, especially in a child under 2 or with a bulging, erythematous TM and systemic features.
Incorrect. Topical ear drops are used for otitis externa or after tympanic membrane perforation; they cannot penetrate an intact TM to treat middle-ear infection.
Incorrect. Immediate myringotomy is reserved for complications (e.g. mastoiditis, meningitis) or failure of medical therapy, not routine AOM.
Incorrect. Watchful waiting can be considered in mild AOM in children >2 years without severe features; this child has bulging TM and fever, making antibiotics appropriate.
Incorrect. IV antibiotics and admission are for complicated AOM (e.g. mastoiditis, intracranial extension), which is not present here.
clinicalPearl: Bulging tympanic membrane with fever in a child under 2 years or with severe symptoms is an indication to start amoxicillin rather than observe.
keyClue: Bulging, erythematous tympanic membrane with fever in a 4-year-old
commonExamTrap: Choosing watchful waiting despite a bulging TM and systemic features — watchful waiting is only appropriate in mild, uncomplicated AOM in older children.
missedConcept: Indications for antibiotic treatment versus watchful waiting in paediatric AOM

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old competitive swimmer presents with a 3-day history of right ear itching and pain that worsens with movement of the pinna. He denies hearing loss or fever. On examination, pulling the pinna causes significant pain, there is erythema and oedema of the ear canal, and minimal white discharge is visible. The tympanic membrane appears normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Otitis externa
Acute otitis media
Cholesteatoma
Otitis media with effusion
Furunculosis of the outer ear
explanation: Otitis externa (swimmer's ear) classically presents with ear canal pain, itching, and discharge following water exposure. The hallmark is tragus/pinna tenderness on movement, distinguishing it from AOM where the tympanic membrane is abnormal but pinna movement is not painful. The normal TM rules out AOM. Cholesteatoma presents with painless foul-smelling discharge and conductive hearing loss.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Otitis externa is characterised by ear canal inflammation with pinna/tragus tenderness on movement, often precipitated by swimming or moisture exposure.
Incorrect. Acute otitis media causes deep ear pain and a bulging or perforated TM; pinna movement does not typically exacerbate pain.
Incorrect. Cholesteatoma presents with chronic painless foul-smelling otorrhoea, hearing loss, and destruction of ossicles — not acute pain in a swimmer.
Incorrect. Otitis media with effusion ('glue ear') is typically painless and presents with conductive hearing loss, not an acutely painful inflamed canal.
Incorrect. Furunculosis is a localised abscess in the outer third of the canal and appears as a discrete, tender boil; it would not cause diffuse canal oedema and discharge.
clinicalPearl: Pinna/tragus tenderness that worsens with movement is the hallmark of otitis externa, distinguishing it from middle-ear pathology.
keyClue: Pain on pinna movement in a swimmer with erythematous canal and normal TM
commonExamTrap: Confusing otitis externa with AOM — in AOM, the pain is deep and not worsened by moving the pinna, and the TM is abnormal.
missedConcept: Clinical distinction between otitis externa and acute otitis media

clinicalStem: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with his second episode this week of spontaneous right-sided nosebleed. He is otherwise healthy and takes no medications. The blood is bright red and flowing from the anterior nasal septum. Vital signs are normal. The bleeding has been ongoing for 10 minutes. On inspection, a prominent vessel is visible at Little's area. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Firm continuous pinching of the soft part of the nose for 10–15 minutes
Posterior nasal packing with a Foley catheter
Immediate chemical cauterisation with silver nitrate
Application of topical adrenaline-soaked gauze followed by anterior packing
Urgent referral to ENT for endoscopic vessel ligation
explanation: Anterior epistaxis, the most common type, is initially managed with direct pressure — firm pinching of the soft part of the nose (not the bony bridge) with the patient leaning forward for 10–15 minutes. This compresses the Kiesselbach plexus at Little's area and stops the majority of cases. Posterior packing, cauterisation, and surgical ligation are escalation steps only if first aid fails. Immediate cauterisation without trying pressure first is not the correct sequence.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Firm continuous pinching of the soft nasal tip for 10–15 minutes is the correct first-line management for anterior epistaxis; most cases resolve with this alone.
Incorrect. Posterior packing is reserved for posterior epistaxis or anterior bleeding that has failed all other measures; it carries risks of hypoxia and is not a first step.
Incorrect. Chemical cauterisation with silver nitrate is appropriate after bleeding is controlled or if a prominent vessel persists, but direct pressure should be attempted first.
Incorrect. Adrenaline-soaked gauze and anterior packing are second-line steps after first aid (pinching) fails, not the initial management.
Incorrect. Endoscopic vessel ligation is reserved for recurrent or severe epistaxis refractory to conservative and packing measures.
clinicalPearl: Anterior epistaxis from Little's area is managed first with firm pinching of the soft nose for 10–15 minutes with the patient leaning forward; escalate only if this fails.
keyClue: Bright-red bleeding from anterior septum (Little's area) visible vessel, stable vital signs
commonExamTrap: Jumping to cauterisation or packing without first attempting simple direct pressure — most anterior epistaxis resolves with firm pinching alone.
missedConcept: Step-wise management of anterior epistaxis: first-aid pressure before any intervention

clinicalStem: A 22-year-old university student presents with 4 days of worsening sore throat, now with severe right-sided throat pain, difficulty swallowing, muffled 'hot potato' voice, and drooling. He is febrile at 38.9°C. On examination, there is unilateral swelling of the right peritonsillar region with uvular deviation to the left, erythema, and trismus. He can open his mouth only partially. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Needle aspiration or incision and drainage of the abscess plus IV antibiotics
Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate for 10 days as outpatient
Immediate tonsillectomy under general anaesthesia
CT scan of the neck prior to any intervention
Corticosteroids alone to reduce peritonsillar swelling
explanation: Peritonsillar abscess (quinsy) requires prompt surgical drainage — needle aspiration or incision and drainage — alongside systemic antibiotics. This relieves the pus collection, resolves trismus, and prevents airway compromise. Oral antibiotics alone are insufficient once an abscess has formed. Immediate tonsillectomy under GA ('quinsy tonsillectomy') is considered if drainage fails or there is airway compromise. CT scan may delay definitive treatment unnecessarily in a clinically clear case.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Needle aspiration or incision and drainage combined with IV antibiotics is the definitive treatment for peritonsillar abscess; drainage addresses the abscess and antibiotics treat systemic infection.
Incorrect. Oral antibiotics alone are appropriate for tonsillitis but insufficient once a frank abscess has formed — pus collections require surgical drainage.
Incorrect. Immediate tonsillectomy under GA is considered for recurrent quinsy or failed drainage, but it is not first-line and carries anaesthetic risk with a partially obstructed airway.
Incorrect. While CT can be useful to confirm diagnosis or plan if atypical, it should not delay drainage in a clinically obvious peritonsillar abscess with trismus.
Incorrect. Corticosteroids may reduce oedema in tonsillitis but cannot drain a formed abscess and are not sufficient as sole management for quinsy.
clinicalPearl: Quinsy is diagnosed clinically by unilateral peritonsillar swelling, uvular deviation, hot-potato voice, and trismus; treatment is immediate drainage plus IV antibiotics.
keyClue: Uvular deviation away from the swollen side with hot-potato voice and trismus
commonExamTrap: Prescribing oral antibiotics alone for quinsy — once an abscess has formed, drainage is mandatory.
missedConcept: Distinction between peritonsillar cellulitis (antibiotics) and peritonsillar abscess (drainage required)
redFlagReason: Peritonsillar abscess with trismus and progressive swelling can cause airway compromise; drainage must not be delayed as the airway may close rapidly.

clinicalStem: A 48-year-old woman presents with a sudden onset of left-sided hearing loss over the past 24 hours, accompanied by mild tinnitus but no vertigo or facial weakness. She denies recent upper respiratory tract infection, head trauma, or ototoxic medication use. Weber test lateralises to the right; Rinne test shows air conduction greater than bone conduction bilaterally. Pure-tone audiometry confirms left-sided sensorineural hearing loss of 45 dB across most frequencies. What is the most important next investigation to exclude a serious underlying cause?
options: MRI of the internal auditory meati with gadolinium contrast
CT scan of the temporal bones
Serum VDRL and FTA-ABS (syphilis serology)
Autoimmune antibody panel (ANA, ANCA)
Auditory brainstem response (ABR) testing only
explanation: Sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL) requires MRI of the internal auditory meati with gadolinium to exclude a vestibular schwannoma (acoustic neuroma), which can present exactly this way. MRI is far superior to CT for soft-tissue lesions of the posterior fossa and internal auditory canal. While syphilis serology and autoimmune panels are part of the workup for SSNHL, the single most important investigation to exclude a space-occupying lesion is gadolinium-enhanced MRI.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. MRI with gadolinium of the internal auditory meati is the most important investigation to rule out vestibular schwannoma, which can present as sudden unilateral SNHL.
Incorrect. CT of the temporal bones is useful for bony pathology (e.g. otosclerosis, fracture) but has poor sensitivity for soft-tissue lesions like acoustic neuroma in the IAC.
Incorrect. Syphilis serology is part of a SSNHL screen but is not the single most important investigation to exclude a dangerous structural cause.
Incorrect. Autoimmune panels are relevant in suspected autoimmune inner ear disease but do not exclude the most dangerous structural cause (vestibular schwannoma).
Incorrect. ABR can suggest retrocochlear pathology but lacks the sensitivity and specificity of MRI; it would not rule out a small acoustic neuroma.
clinicalPearl: Any patient with unilateral sudden SNHL must have gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the internal auditory meati to exclude vestibular schwannoma before attributing the loss to idiopathic causes.
keyClue: Sudden unilateral SNHL confirmed on audiometry with Weber lateralising away from the affected ear
commonExamTrap: Ordering CT instead of MRI — CT cannot reliably visualise the soft-tissue contents of the internal auditory canal.
missedConcept: Vestibular schwannoma as a cause of unilateral SNHL and the role of gadolinium MRI in its exclusion

clinicalStem: A 35-year-old man presents with 8 days of facial pain and pressure predominantly over his cheeks, purulent nasal discharge, nasal congestion, and reduced smell. He had a viral URTI 10 days ago. He is afebrile. Maxillary tenderness is present on palpation. There are no neurological symptoms, no periorbital swelling, and no severe headache. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Intranasal saline irrigation, topical decongestants, and analgesia with reassurance
Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate for 10–14 days immediately
CT scan of the paranasal sinuses
Urgent ENT referral for sinus washout
Oral corticosteroids for 5 days
explanation: Acute rhinosinusitis lasting less than 10–12 days following a viral URTI is most commonly viral in aetiology. Current guidelines recommend saline nasal irrigation, topical decongestants, and analgesia as first-line treatment; antibiotics are not routinely indicated unless symptoms persist beyond 10 days without improvement, worsen after initial improvement, or there are features of severe bacterial sinusitis. There are no complications (orbital or intracranial) here to warrant imaging or urgent referral.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. For uncomplicated acute viral rhinosinusitis, symptom management with saline irrigation, decongestants, and analgesia is first-line; antibiotics are not routinely indicated in the first 10 days.
Incorrect. Antibiotics are not first-line for acute sinusitis of less than 10 days' duration following a viral URTI; most cases are viral and self-limiting.
Incorrect. CT sinuses are indicated for suspected complications (orbital cellulitis, intracranial extension) or chronic/recurrent sinusitis — not routine acute uncomplicated sinusitis.
Incorrect. Sinus washout (antral puncture or FESS) is reserved for chronic sinusitis or sinusitis refractory to medical therapy, not acute uncomplicated cases.
Incorrect. Oral corticosteroids alone are not standard first-line treatment for acute sinusitis; intranasal steroids may have a role but systemic steroids are not routinely indicated.
clinicalPearl: Acute sinusitis following a viral URTI is managed conservatively for the first 10 days; antibiotics are reserved for persistent, worsening, or severe/complicated cases.
keyClue: Facial pain and purulent discharge 8–10 days after viral URTI with no complications
commonExamTrap: Immediately prescribing antibiotics for all sinusitis — most acute cases are viral and antibiotic therapy should be delayed unless bacterial features or complications are present.
missedConcept: Conservative management of uncomplicated viral rhinosinusitis before antibiotic use

clinicalStem: An 18-month-old girl is brought in by her father after a sudden onset of choking, coughing, and respiratory distress while playing with small toy parts 30 minutes ago. On arrival she is conscious, distressed, and showing inspiratory stridor, tachypnoea (RR 44), and decreased breath sounds at the right lung base. She is maintaining her oxygen saturation at 92% on room air. Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation of the right lung. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Urgent rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia for foreign body removal
Back blows and abdominal thrusts (Heimlich manoeuvre) immediately
Flexible bronchoscopy as a diagnostic and therapeutic procedure
High-flow oxygen and urgent CT chest to localise the foreign body
Blind finger sweep of the oropharynx and nasopharynx
explanation: Suspected inhaled foreign body in a child with respiratory distress, stridor, unilateral decreased breath sounds, and unilateral hyperinflation on CXR (ball-valve mechanism) is a surgical emergency requiring urgent rigid bronchoscopy under GA. Rigid bronchoscopy is the gold standard for removal of airway foreign bodies in children as it allows airway control, excellent visualisation, and use of large forceps. Heimlich manoeuvre is for complete airway obstruction. Flexible bronchoscopy is less safe for removal in small children.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia is the definitive and gold-standard management for confirmed or highly suspected endobronchial foreign body in children.

Incorrect. Back blows and Heimlich manoeuvre are first-aid measures for complete airway obstruction in a conscious patient; this child has a partial obstruction with maintained consciousness and SpO2 — emergency bronchoscopy is the appropriate step.
Incorrect. Flexible bronchoscopy lacks the airway control, large-bore instruments, and safety margin needed for FB removal in young children; rigid bronchoscopy under GA is preferred.

Incorrect. CT chest may further confirm and localise a foreign body but delays definitive treatment; the clinical and radiological picture here is already strongly indicative and bronchoscopy should not wait for CT.
Incorrect. Blind finger sweeps are contraindicated even in complete obstruction unless the object is clearly visible; in partial obstruction they risk converting it to a complete obstruction.
clinicalPearl: Unilateral hyperinflation on CXR after a choking episode in a toddler is a foreign body until proven otherwise — proceed directly to rigid bronchoscopy without delay.
keyClue: Sudden choking in a toddler with unilateral hyperinflation on CXR and decreased ipsilateral breath sounds
commonExamTrap: Ordering CT before bronchoscopy — imaging should not delay definitive airway management in a deteriorating child with foreign body aspiration.
missedConcept: Rigid bronchoscopy as the gold standard for endobronchial foreign body removal in children
redFlagReason: Inhaled foreign body with partial airway obstruction and hypoxia (SpO2 92%) in an 18-month-old can rapidly progress to complete obstruction and respiratory failure; delay in definitive management is life-threatening.

clinicalStem: A 6-year-old boy is referred by his school teacher because of inattentiveness and apparent difficulty hearing. His mother reports he has had recurrent ear infections. Otoscopy shows a retracted, dull, amber tympanic membrane bilaterally with a fluid level visible on the right. Pure-tone audiometry reveals bilateral conductive hearing loss of 35 dB. Tympanometry shows bilateral type B (flat) curves. The condition has been present for at least 3 months. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Insertion of ventilation tubes (grommets) bilaterally
Prolonged course of oral amoxicillin for 3 months
Watchful waiting for a further 3 months
Adenoidectomy alone without grommets
Hearing aids as definitive treatment
explanation: Otitis media with effusion (glue ear) persisting for 3 months or more with bilateral conductive hearing loss ≥25–30 dB affecting speech, language, or school performance is an indication for surgical intervention. Grommet (ventilation tube) insertion equalises middle-ear pressure, allows fluid drainage, and restores hearing. Antibiotics are not indicated for glue ear. Watchful waiting is appropriate for shorter durations; at 3 months with documented hearing impact and school difficulties, surgical management is warranted.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Bilateral grommet insertion is indicated for persistent OME (≥3 months) with significant bilateral conductive hearing loss causing educational or developmental impact.
Incorrect. Antibiotics are not recommended for OME; glue ear is not an acute bacterial infection and antibiotics do not resolve the effusion.
Incorrect. Further watchful waiting is not appropriate when the condition has already been present for 3 months with documented hearing loss affecting school performance.

Incorrect. Adenoidectomy alone (without grommets) may reduce recurrence but is not the primary intervention for established bilateral OME with significant hearing loss; grommets address the immediate hearing deficit.

Incorrect. Hearing aids are an alternative if surgery is declined or contraindicated, but grommet insertion is the preferred surgical standard of care for eligible children with persistent OME and hearing loss.
clinicalPearl: OME (glue ear) persisting beyond 3 months with bilateral conductive hearing loss ≥25 dB causing educational impact is an indication for grommet insertion.
keyClue: 3 months of bilateral conductive hearing loss with type B tympanogram and school difficulties in a 6-year-old
commonExamTrap: Prescribing antibiotics for glue ear — OME is not an acute bacterial infection and antibiotics are not indicated.
missedConcept: Indications for grommet insertion in otitis media with effusion

clinicalStem: A 10-year-old girl is reviewed in ENT outpatients. Her parents report she has had 7 confirmed episodes of acute tonsillitis requiring antibiotics in the past 12 months, with 4 episodes in the year before. Each episode involves high fever, exudative tonsils, and significant school absence. On examination today she is well, with mildly enlarged tonsils but no peritonsillar swelling. Her tonsils are graded T2. She has no contraindications to surgery. According to standard guidelines, what is the most appropriate recommendation?
options: Tonsillectomy is indicated and should be offered
Continue antibiotics prophylactically and review in 6 months
Wait for a further 2 years before considering surgery
Adenoidectomy alone without tonsillectomy
Tonsil size alone determines the need for surgery; T2 tonsils do not require removal
explanation: The Paradise criteria (widely adopted) recommend tonsillectomy when a child has ≥7 episodes in the preceding year, or ≥5 per year over 2 years, or ≥3 per year over 3 years — all severe and documented. This child meets the ≥7-episodes-in-one-year criterion. Surgery significantly reduces recurrence, school absence, and antibiotic use. Prophylactic antibiotics are not standard. Tonsil size alone is not a primary surgical indication; it is the frequency and severity of infection that drive the decision.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. This child meets Paradise criteria for tonsillectomy (≥7 episodes in one year), and tonsillectomy is the appropriate recommendation.
Incorrect. Antibiotic prophylaxis is not standard of care for recurrent tonsillitis and does not address the underlying frequency of episodes; it also risks resistance.
Incorrect. Further waiting is not appropriate when established criteria have already been met; continued episodes cause school absence and morbidity.
Incorrect. Adenoidectomy alone may help with nasal obstruction or OME but does not adequately address recurrent tonsillitis; tonsillectomy is required.
Incorrect. Tonsil size (grading) determines obstruction-based indications (e.g. sleep apnoea); recurrent infection indications are frequency-based, not size-based.
clinicalPearl: Tonsillectomy is indicated when a child has ≥7 documented tonsillitis episodes in one year, ≥5/year over two years, or ≥3/year over three years (Paradise criteria).
keyClue: 7 confirmed episodes of acute tonsillitis in the past 12 months
commonExamTrap: Basing the decision on tonsil size rather than frequency and severity of infection — Paradise criteria are frequency-based.
missedConcept: Paradise criteria for tonsillectomy in children with recurrent tonsillitis

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of a painless, slowly enlarging lump in the anterior neck. She has no dysphagia, hoarseness, or respiratory symptoms. On examination there is a 2.5 cm firm, non-tender nodule in the right lobe of the thyroid that moves upward on swallowing. Cervical lymphadenopathy is not palpable. TFTs are normal. What is the single most appropriate next investigation?
options: Ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) of the thyroid nodule
CT scan of the neck with contrast
Thyroid radionuclide (technetium-99m) scintigraphy
Serum thyroglobulin level
Surgical excision of the nodule without prior cytology
explanation: A solitary thyroid nodule in a middle-aged woman with normal TFTs requires ultrasound-guided FNAC as the single most informative investigation for cytological diagnosis. FNAC categorises the nodule using the Thy classification (Thy1–5) and guides management decisions. CT is useful for staging if malignancy is confirmed but is not the first investigation. Scintigraphy shows functional status (hot vs cold) but 'cold' nodules still need cytology. Thyroglobulin is a tumour marker used for monitoring after treatment, not primary diagnosis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Ultrasound-guided FNAC is the gold-standard first investigation for a solitary thyroid nodule with normal TFTs; it provides cytological diagnosis and guides management.
Incorrect. CT neck may be used for staging or pre-operative planning in confirmed thyroid malignancy, but it cannot provide a cytological diagnosis and is not the first-line investigation.

Incorrect. Radionuclide scintigraphy identifies functional status; however, even 'cold' (non-functioning) nodules require cytological confirmation as they carry a ~5–15% malignancy risk — FNAC is still necessary.
Incorrect. Serum thyroglobulin is used for surveillance after total thyroidectomy in differentiated thyroid cancer, not for diagnosis of an initial nodule.
Incorrect. Excision without prior cytology removes the benefit of tissue diagnosis; FNAC may avoid surgery in benign lesions and guides the extent of surgery when malignancy is confirmed.
clinicalPearl: All thyroid nodules with normal TFTs should undergo ultrasound and FNAC; scintigraphy is reserved for nodules associated with a suppressed TSH to look for hyperfunctioning adenoma.
keyClue: Firm nodule moving with swallowing, normal TFTs, no lymphadenopathy — solitary thyroid nodule requiring cytological diagnosis
commonExamTrap: Ordering scintigraphy instead of FNAC — scintigraphy does not provide cytology and 'cold' nodules still require FNAC.
missedConcept: FNAC as the first-line investigation for solitary thyroid nodule with normal TFTs

clinicalStem: A 3-year-old unvaccinated boy is brought to the emergency department by ambulance with a 4-hour history of fever (40.1°C), rapidly worsening stridor, drooling, and refusal to eat or drink. He is sitting upright leaning forward with neck extended ('sniffing position'), appears anxious and toxic. He has a muffled cry. His oxygen saturation is 94% on air. The parents mention he has not received the Hib vaccine. What is the most critical immediate step in management?
options: Urgent senior anaesthetist and ENT review for controlled intubation in the operating theatre
Direct laryngoscopy in the emergency department to visualise the epiglottis
Lateral neck X-ray to confirm the 'thumbprint sign'
Administering high-flow oxygen via face mask immediately
IV access, blood cultures, and immediate IV cefotaxime
explanation: Acute epiglottitis in a child is a life-threatening airway emergency. The airway can close completely at any moment. The MOST critical step is not to disturb the child but to urgently arrange controlled intubation in an operating theatre environment with a senior anaesthetist and ENT surgeon present, who can perform an emergency surgical airway if intubation fails. Any attempt to examine the mouth or perform laryngoscopy in the ED may cause complete laryngospasm. Imaging delays definitive airway management. IV access and antibiotics come after the airway is secured.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. The immediate priority in suspected epiglottitis is airway safety — urgent controlled intubation in theatre by experienced anaesthetics and ENT avoids precipitating complete obstruction by distressing the child.

Incorrect. Direct laryngoscopy in the emergency department is absolutely contraindicated in suspected epiglottitis; stimulating the inflamed epiglottis can trigger complete laryngospasm and fatal airway obstruction.
Incorrect. Lateral neck X-ray (showing 'thumbprint sign') may support the diagnosis but delays airway management; the diagnosis is clinical and imaging must not delay securing the airway.

Incorrect. While supplemental oxygen is needed, it must be delivered in a non-distressing way (e.g. 'blow-by') without agitating the child; this is an adjunct, not the most critical step — securing the airway takes precedence.

Incorrect. IV access, blood cultures, and antibiotics are important steps but come AFTER the airway is secured; attempting IV cannulation in a distressed child before the airway is controlled risks precipitating complete obstruction.
clinicalPearl: In suspected epiglottitis, never examine the throat or perform laryngoscopy in the ED — keep the child calm, administer blow-by oxygen, and urgently transfer to theatre for controlled intubation.
keyClue: Unvaccinated toddler in 'sniffing position' with drooling, muffled cry, rapidly progressive stridor, and high fever
commonExamTrap: Ordering a neck X-ray or attempting direct laryngoscopy before securing the airway — both can delay or precipitate fatal airway obstruction.
missedConcept: Airway management in epiglottitis takes absolute priority; never examine the oropharynx or delay intubation for investigations
redFlagReason: Acute epiglottitis in a child can cause complete airway obstruction within minutes; any distressing procedure in the ED risks fatal laryngospasm before the airway is secured.

clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman presents with progressive bilateral hearing loss over 5 years, worse in the left ear. She finds she can hear better in noisy environments than in quiet ones (paracusis Willisii). She is 6 weeks postpartum; she also had temporary deterioration of hearing during her previous pregnancy. Her family history includes a grandmother with hearing aids. On otoscopy, both tympanic membranes appear normal. Audiometry reveals bilateral low-frequency conductive hearing loss. Tympanometry shows a type As (shallow/stiffened) curve. The Rinne test shows bone conduction greater than air conduction bilaterally. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Otosclerosis
Otitis media with effusion
Presbycusis
Noise-induced hearing loss
Cholesteatoma
explanation: Otosclerosis is an autosomal dominant condition causing abnormal bone remodelling at the oval window (stapes fixation), resulting in progressive bilateral conductive hearing loss. Key features: young adult woman, bilateral low-frequency conductive hearing loss, normal TMs, type As tympanogram (reduced compliance due to stapes fixation), paracusis Willisii (hearing better in noise), and worsening during pregnancy. OME would show type B tympanogram and is usually associated with catarrhal symptoms. Presbycusis is sensorineural and occurs in the elderly.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Otosclerosis presents in young adult women with bilateral progressive conductive hearing loss, normal TMs, type As tympanogram, paracusis Willisii, and worsening during pregnancy — all features present here.
Incorrect. OME causes type B (flat) tympanogram (not type As) and is typically associated with upper respiratory tract infection or Eustachian tube dysfunction; paracusis Willisii is not a feature.
Incorrect. Presbycusis is age-related sensorineural hearing loss affecting high frequencies in older individuals; it does not cause conductive loss and is not present in a 34-year-old.
Incorrect. Noise-induced hearing loss is sensorineural (SNHL), typically affects high frequencies (4 kHz notch), and is related to occupational or recreational noise exposure — not conductive.
Incorrect. Cholesteatoma causes conductive hearing loss but is associated with a retraction pocket or perforation on otoscopy, and often painless foul-smelling discharge — not a normal TM.
clinicalPearl: Otosclerosis should be suspected in a young woman with bilateral progressive conductive hearing loss, normal tympanic membranes, type As tympanogram, paracusis Willisii, and pregnancy-related exacerbation.
keyClue: Young woman with bilateral conductive hearing loss, normal TMs, type As tympanogram, paracusis Willisii, and worsening in pregnancy
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing OME because of bilateral conductive hearing loss — the type As (stiff) tympanogram distinguishes otosclerosis (reduced compliance) from OME (type B, flat).
missedConcept: Otosclerosis: clinical features including paracusis Willisii, type As tympanogram, and pregnancy-related exacerbation
